name: Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez Hiking Trail
name_original: Ruta de la Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez desde Coria
description: Hike the moderate 7.8-mile Route of the Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez from Coria, featuring diverse landscapes and valley views.
region_intro: Hiking around Coria offers diverse landscapes, from the fertile Alagón Valley to the foothills of the Sierra de Gata mountains. The region is characterized by river views, dense forests of cork oaks, chestnuts, and holm oaks, and varied terrain including crags and meadows. Trails often follow ancient paths, integrating natural beauty with historical landmarks. Best hiking trails around Coria The most popular hiking route is Coria to Ermita Virgen de Argeme, a 6.6 miles (10.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 46 minutes to complete. This route offers river views and passes cultural landmarks. Another top favourite among local hikers is Route of the Canals, a moderate 5.2 miles (8.4 km) path. It provides panoramas of the historic town and views of the surrounding natural features. Local hikers also love the Rincón del Obispo Trail, a 6.7 miles (10.8 km) trail leading through the Alagón Valley, often completed in about 2 hours 47 minutes. Hiking around Coria is defined by river plains, diverse woodlands, and mountain foothills. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Coria are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from 7 reviews. More than 60 hikers have used komoot to explore Coria's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking around Coria?
A: Hiking around Coria offers a diverse range of landscapes, from the fertile Alagón Valley with its river views and riverside forests to the foothills of the Sierra de Gata mountains. You'll encounter dense forests of cork oaks, strawberry trees, oaks, and chestnuts, as well as meadows, crags, and mountain passes. The area is known for its rich tapestry of natural features.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Coria?
A: Yes, Coria offers several easy hiking trails. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1273845879/coria-to-ermita-virgen-de-argeme" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coria to Ermita Virgen de Argeme&lt;/a&gt; route is a moderate trail that provides river views and passes cultural landmarks, making it accessible for many. Overall, there are 6 easy routes available in the area.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in the Coria area?
A: Yes, circular routes are available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1276610506/route-of-the-canals" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Canals&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate circular hike offering panoramas of the historic town and views of the surrounding natural features.

Q: Are there any trails that pass by waterfalls or other significant natural features?
A: Yes, the region boasts beautiful natural features. You can find the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5431398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chorrerón Waterfall and Stone Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, which is a notable highlight in the area. The Sierra de Gata also features rivers that rarely run dry, offering refreshing natural pools.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I explore on hikes near Coria?
A: Many trails in Coria integrate with its rich history. You can encounter ancient city walls, Roman funeral monuments, and medieval bridges. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5430399" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Bridge of Coria&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6047850" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Bridge of Coria&lt;/a&gt; are significant landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1273776345/via-dalmacia-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Dalmacia Trail&lt;/a&gt; is another route with historical significance.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Coria?
A: The Sierra de Gata, located near Coria, is particularly delightful for walks in autumn due to its slopes covered in deciduous trees. However, the region's rivers rarely run dry, even in summer, providing refreshing natural pools. Generally, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for hiking.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options around Coria?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' routes aren't explicitly categorized, many of the easier and moderate trails, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1275423378/rincon-del-obispo-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rincón del Obispo Trail&lt;/a&gt;, are suitable for families looking for a pleasant walk through the Alagón Valley. Coria also has riverside parks like La Isla Riverside Park, which blend nature with leisure, ideal for strolls with children.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I see while hiking in Coria?
A: The Sierra de Gata mountains are home to a rich diversity of fauna, including large birds of prey like Eurasian black vultures and griffon vultures. The varied forests and river ecosystems also support a range of other wildlife, making for interesting observations during your hike.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Coria?
A: The hiking routes in Coria are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from 7 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from river plains to mountain foothills, and the integration of natural beauty with historical landmarks along the trails.

Q: Are there any longer or more challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, there is at least one difficult route available in the Coria area. The region's proximity to the Sierra de Gata mountains, including peaks like Pico Jálama (1,487 meters), offers opportunities for more strenuous climbs and spectacular regional views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1276543948/dehesa-boyal-de-minguez-hiking-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez Hiking Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a longer moderate option.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads around Coria?
A: While specific parking details for individual trailheads are not provided, Coria, as a town, offers various parking options. For routes starting directly from Coria, you can typically find parking within the town or near its immediate outskirts, especially for trails like those connecting to the Ermita Virgen de Argeme.

Q: Can I reach hiking trails in Coria using public transport?
A: Coria is a regional hub, and while direct public transport to every trailhead might be limited, the town itself is accessible. Many trails, such as those within Coria's municipal territory or leading from the town, can be accessed on foot from public transport stops within Coria.
tips: Founded before the Romans occupied the Iberian Peninsula, Coria was known as Cauria or Caurus. The Romans gave it its current Latin name, CAVRIUM. Later, he was granted Roman citizenship. In the Visigothic era, the Diocese of Coria was created, which maintained Coria as an episcopal see until the 20th century. After the Reconquista, Coria became the capital of a manor to which towns that still bear the name of Coria belonged, such as Guijo de Coria or Casillas de Coria.

The Iron Bridge in Coria is a beautiful civil work of metal architecture and nineteenth-century modernist design. It was built by the Asturian engineers D. Salvador and D. Próspero Ardura. The bridge stands over the Alagón River, connecting both banks in the City of Coria. Its total length is 178.30 meters, and its deck has a width of 7.78 meters, with a two-way road flanked by two sidewalks.

This natural viewpoint is located about 9 kilometers from Coria. From here you can see the beautiful Valle del Alagón, the Sierra de Gata, where the Pico Jálama stands out, and even the Sierra de la Estrella in Portugal.

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike the Route of the Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez from Coria, and is it a loop?
faqs.answer: This route is a linear (out and back) trail, not a loop. It typically takes about 2 hours and 30 minutes to complete the full 12.5 km (7.8 miles) round trip.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level and terrain like on this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is rated as moderate. It starts on flat terrain through irrigated farmlands. As you approach the Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez, the path becomes winding with a continuous uphill slope, characterized by holm oaks and sharp slate rock formations.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and points of interest can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse scenery, including riverine environments, agricultural fields, pastures, and Mediterranean forest (dehesa). Key highlights include the historic city center of Coria, the Iron Bridge of Coria, and the Alto de la Dehesa de Mínguez Viewpoint, which offers expansive vistas of the Alagón Valley with the Sierra de Gata and Sierra de Gredos in the background. You can also spot traditional constructions like shepherd's huts and fountains.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez from Coria dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near livestock, and to respect local regulations regarding pets in natural spaces.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to hike are typically spring and autumn. During these seasons, the temperatures are milder, and the natural landscape is vibrant. Summers can be very hot in this region, and winters might bring cooler, wetter conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to access the Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail or access the Dehesa Boyal de Mínguez. It's a publicly accessible municipal property.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead in Coria?
faqs.answer: The trail starts from Coria. While specific parking information isn't detailed, you can typically find parking within the town of Coria, especially near the Coria Historic Center, which is a good starting point for the route.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of the route overlap with other paths. You'll find it intersects with parts of the Pista del Canal. Margen Izquierda and the Camino de la Ermita.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a hike on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and continuous uphill section, we recommend bringing plenty of water, appropriate hiking footwear, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and snacks. A camera would also be great for capturing the scenic views from the viewpoint.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: While the initial section is flat, the continuous uphill slope and winding path with slate rock formations make it moderately challenging. It might be suitable for active families with older children, but younger children or those less accustomed to hiking might find the uphill section demanding.

faqs.question: Are there any restaurants or cafes near the trailhead in Coria?
faqs.answer: Yes, Coria is a town with amenities. You'll find various restaurants, cafes, and bars within the town, especially around the Coria Historic Center, perfect for a meal or refreshment before or after your hike.

waypoints.name: Alto de la Dehesa de Mínguez Viewpoint
